sourceCitation.kuhnCitation: Robert Lawrence Kuhn. "A Landscape of Consciousness: Toward a Taxonomy of Explanations and Implications. Progress in Biophysics and Molecular Biology, Volume 190, 2024, Pages 28-169."
about.heading: The Consciousness Atlas
about.subtitle: Visualizing the Landscape of Consciousness
about.intro.html: The Consciousness Atlas is an interactive web app that visualizes more than 325 theories of consciousness. It is based on Robert Lawrence Kuhn's 2024 paper, "A Landscape of Consciousness". The Atlas maps theories that address phenomenal consciousness - the subjective feel of experience, such as the redness of red.
about.whatIs.title: What the Atlas is
about.whatIs.body: The Atlas is a visual reference built on Kuhn's taxonomy. It presents the full set of theories at once so you can explore the field visually and open any theory to read its entry, like an encyclopedia with structured entries. Each theory entry shows a concise identity line, conceptual ground, suggested mechanism, empirical notes, implications, and sources.
about.why.title: Why this exists
about.why.body: Consciousness is studied across disciplines - neuroscience, philosophy, and sometimes spiritual or anomalous-state research. Kuhn's paper collects and organizes hundreds of theories along a spectrum from most physical to least physical. The Atlas puts that organization into a visual form so readers, students, and researchers can quickly see how different positions relate and where each theory sits on the spectrum.
about.how.title: How it works
about.how.body: Theories are grouped into ten main categories, arranged by their stance on the physical-to-nonphysical spectrum. Examples of categories include Materialism, Non-Reductive Physicalism, Functionalism, Quantum-based theories, Panpsychism, Monism, Dualism, Idealism, Anomalous States, and Challenge Theories. The app shows the whole map at once. Click any theory to read its structured entry, which includes references to canonical sources and named thinkers.
about.audience.title: Who it is for
about.audience.item1: Students learning philosophy of mind and cognitive science
about.audience.item2: Researchers and lecturers looking for an at-a-glance comparative resource
about.audience.item3: Journalists and writers who need a quick reference to the range of positions
about.audience.item4: Curious readers who want a clear view of what people mean when they talk about consciousness
about.notDo.title: What the Atlas does not do
about.notDo.body: The Atlas does not endorse or rank theories. It is a visualization and reference based on Kuhn's taxonomy - collect and present, not judge. It does not attempt to settle the hard problem of consciousness. It is a tool for exploration and comparison.
about.kuhnNote.title: A note from Kuhn
about.kuhnNote.body1: Kuhn explicitly states the project's purpose: "collect and categorize, not assess and adjudicate." The Consciousness Atlas follows that approach. The data and structure are derived from Kuhn's paper; feedback and suggested corrections are welcome, but the app is a visualization layer on top of Kuhn's work.
about.kuhnNote.body2: Kuhn was not involved in the design or content of the Consciousness Atlas, other than his original paper, 'A Landscape of Consciousness,' which is the sole source.
about.landscapeWebsite.title: Landscape of Consciousness Website
about.landscapeWebsite.body.html: The Landscape of Consciousness website is a significant extension, enhancement, and update of Robert Lawrence Kuhn's paper, "A Landscape of Consciousness: Toward a Taxonomy of Explanations and Implications." The Landscape of Consciousness website contains over 350 theories of consciousness that can be accessed in five ways: Landscape Grid; Landscape Categories; All Landscape Theories; Landscape Map; Interactive Visualizations. The Landscape of Consciousness is a work-in-progress – permanently.
about.faq.title: FAQ
about.faq.a1: Phenomenal consciousness refers to subjective experience - the qualitative feel of being aware, like the taste of coffee or the redness of red.
about.faq.q2: How many theories does the Atlas include?
about.faq.a2: The Atlas visualizes over 220 individual theories, grouped into ten broad categories.
about.faq.q3: Does the Atlas show which theory is true?
about.faq.a3: No. The Atlas presents theories neutrally. It is a map, not an argument for any single position.
about.faq.a4: The Atlas is based on an academic source - Robert Kuhn's 2024 paper, "A Landscape of Consciousness" - and links to canonical sources where available.
about.related.title: Related reading
about.related.body.html: The Atlas references canonical research across philosophy and neuroscience, including Integrated Information Theory, Global Workspace Theory, panpsychist proposals, quantum-based ideas, and historical positions from classical thinkers. For the original academic context, see Kuhn's article on ScienceDirect: A Landscape of Consciousness.
about.summary.title: Summary
about.summary.body: The Consciousness Atlas is a straightforward visualization tool: it presents Kuhn's taxonomy as a single, explorable map and lets users open individual theory entries to read structured, referenced summaries. It is designed to make this complex field easier to survey and to serve as a practical reference.
about.footer.creatorBody.html: ConsciousnessAtlas.com is an independent project by Danilo Znamerovszkij (LinkedIn), a frontend engineer with experience building interactive tools and data visualizations. The project reflects a long-standing interest in consciousness studies, philosophy of mind, and how complex theoretical landscapes can be made more navigable through software.
about.footer.contactBody.prefix: For feedback, corrections, or research-related inquiries:
about.footer.scopeBody: This project is intended as an exploratory and educational resource. It does not advocate for any particular theory of consciousness and should not be interpreted as an authoritative scientific classification.
